posts.title: The False Promise of 'Frictionless'
posts.content:
# The False Promise of 'Frictionless'

_In high-stakes environments, friction is a feature, not a bug. Removing it entirely often delegates the cognitive load from the interface to the user's anxiety._

## On materials

We treat pixels as if they cost nothing. They cost attention, which is the only currency in interface design. Every glyph, every rule, every margin is a withdrawal from the reader's account. The discipline is to make each withdrawal earn its keep.

> The detail is not the detail. The detail makes the design. — Charles Eames

What follows is a working set of principles I've returned to across the last decade of building tools for specialists. None are original. All are hard-won.
posts.excerpt: In high-stakes environments, friction is a feature, not a bug. Removing it entirely often delegates the cognitive load from the interface to the user's anxiety.

posts.title: On Marginalia: Designing for the Edges
posts.content:
# On Marginalia: Designing for the Edges

_When we build enterprise software, the most important work often happens in the notes fields, the workarounds, and the sticky notes on the monitor. How do we design for the margins?_

The most reliable insights came from the moments when operators reached for paper, opened a second window, or muttered under their breath. Those are the seams in the existing interface, and they pointed precisely where the redesign needed to begin.
posts.excerpt: When we build enterprise software, the most important work often happens in the notes fields, the workarounds, and the sticky notes on the monitor.

posts.title: Syntax over Semantics in Component Libraries
posts.content:
# Syntax over Semantics in Component Libraries

_A critical look at why rigid semantic naming conventions in design systems inevitably collapse under the weight of product reality._

Naming is the second-hardest problem in software, and design systems make it harder by insisting that one name must serve every product surface. The same component is a card here, a tile there, a panel in the third place. Pretending otherwise is a small act of dishonesty that compounds over a release cycle.
posts.excerpt: A critical look at why rigid semantic naming conventions in design systems inevitably collapse under the weight of product reality.

posts.title: The Architecture of Silence in UI
posts.content:
# The Architecture of Silence in UI

_What we choose not to render is as important as what we do. A study of negative space, deliberate omission, and the typography of restraint._

A well-set paragraph delivers more comprehension per square inch than a kanban board ever will, because it exploits a system the reader has spent decades training: the act of reading prose.
posts.excerpt: What we choose not to render is as important as what we do. A study of negative space, deliberate omission, and the typography of restraint.

pages.title: About
pages.content:
I'm a product designer and researcher based in Oslo. For the last decade I've worked at the intersection of complex technical domains and human attention — building the quiet, capable interfaces that specialists rely on without thinking about.

My background is in industrial design and information architecture. Before software, I spent four years designing surgical instruments, which taught me that a good tool disappears in the hand. I try to build software the same way.

Currently I lead product design at **Kinetis Medical**, where we are rebuilding the triage stack used across forty hospital networks. Before Kinetis I led design at Oculus Logistics and consulted independently for ledger and identity protocols.

## Currently

- Reading *The Craftsman* by Richard Sennett
- Listening to Nils Frahm, *Spaces*
- Building a small workshop in the garden
pages.excerpt: About Elias Thorne — product designer and researcher.

pages.title: Oculus Logistics Framework
pages.content:
## The brief

The team had outgrown its original mental model. What began as a focused tool for a small set of operators had calcified into a sprawling surface where every workflow competed for attention. Our charter was to find the underlying structure and rebuild the interface around it.

## Approach

We started with two weeks of shadowing. Not interviews — observation. The most reliable insights came from the moments when operators reached for paper, opened a second window, or muttered under their breath. Those are the seams in the existing interface, and they pointed precisely where the redesign needed to begin.

## Outcome

Twelve weeks later we shipped a system organized around the operator's actual unit of work, rather than the database's unit of storage. Adoption hit ninety-four percent in the first month, error rates dropped by nearly half, and — perhaps most telling — support tickets stopped using the word "confusing."
pages.excerpt: Orchestrating global freight movement through a unified tracking primitive. We reduced dispatch errors by 41% by treating physical containers as state machines rather than static assets.

pages.title: Vanguard Protocol
pages.content:
## The brief

Redesigning the reconciliation engine for decentralized ledgers. The challenge was making cryptographic finality legible to institutional compliance officers without abstracting away the underlying risk mechanics.

## Approach

The research surfaced a recurring tension: compliance teams wanted certainty, while engineering wanted to expose probabilistic guarantees as they actually are. We reframed the question as a typographic one — what does honest uncertainty look like, in a number and in a chart, when the reader is a senior auditor?

## Outcome

The new reconciliation surface separated finality state from operational state, and made the wait visible rather than hiding it behind spinners. Auditor onboarding time dropped from six weeks to nine days.
pages.excerpt: Redesigning the reconciliation engine for decentralized ledgers. The challenge was making cryptographic finality legible to institutional compliance officers without abstracting away the underlying risk mechanics.

pages.title: Kinetis Medical Suite
pages.content:
## The brief

A quiet, capable interface for acute care environments. Translating specialized clinical workflows into a calm, focused triage experience used across forty hospital networks.

## Approach

We shadowed clinical teams across three regions, paying attention to the moments where the interface forced the operator to break their attention from the patient. Each break became a candidate for redesign.

## Outcome

We shipped a triage stack that consolidated three separate tools into a single, calmer surface. Time-to-decision improved measurably; more importantly, clinicians described the new interface as "out of the way."
pages.excerpt: A quiet, capable interface for acute care environments. Translating specialized clinical workflows into a calm, focused triage experience used across forty hospital networks.
